sources.note: History of Cornelius family; section 3-VI gives Elias Cornelius D.D. line

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the man his father succeeded
narrative_blocks.body: His father christened him for Jeremiah Evarts, the missionary-board secretary whose office the elder Cornelius had inherited. He sold life insurance in Boston; his son Robert died at eleven.

sources.note: History of Cornelius family; section 3-VI gives Elias Cornelius D.D. line

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the man his father succeeded
narrative_blocks.body: His father christened him for Jeremiah Evarts, the missionary-board secretary whose office the elder Cornelius had inherited. He sold life insurance in Boston; his son Robert died at eleven.

institutions.institution_blurb: Boston University School of Law LL.B. 1889. Admitted to bar 1889.

narrative_blocks.header: He served on Boston Common Council
narrative_blocks.body: John Henry Colby began practice with his father in 1889, continued until his father's death in 1890, then formed the firm Colby & Bayley. He served on Boston Common Council 1893-1895, the Board of Aldermen 1897-1899, and the Massachusetts legislature in 1900. He was also clerk and trustee of the North End Savings Bank and director of the Boston City Missionary Society.

narrative_blocks.header: He died suddenly at the summer home
narrative_blocks.body: On September 11, 1909, John Henry Colby died suddenly of heart disease at the family's summer home in Hanover, New Hampshire. His son John Noyes was fifteen. Seven years later, John Noyes died in the same town — at Dartmouth's Mary Hitchcock Memorial Hospital — at age 22.

institutions.institution_blurb: Boston Latin School class of 1912 — the school's largest senior class to that date (102 members). Award for excellence in modern studies 1908.
Dartmouth College class of 1916 (A.B.). Football letter man three years; president Dartmouth College Club and class of '16; Beta Theta Pi.

narrative_blocks.header: He died eight days before his 23rd birthday
narrative_blocks.body: John Noyes Colby graduated from Dartmouth in 1916 — class president, football letter man, Beta Theta Pi. He enrolled in a post-graduate course at the Tuck Hospital in Hanover. In early November he contracted pneumonia, recovered enough to spend Thanksgiving in Boston, returned to Hanover on December 3, relapsed, and died on December 7, 1916 at Mary Hitchcock Memorial Hospital.

John Noyes Colby entered Dartmouth following his father John Henry Colby (class of 1885) and his grandfather John Freeman Colby (class of 1859). Three consecutive generations of the same family at the same college, each a decade and a half apart. John Noyes graduated in 1916 — the year he died.

narrative_blocks.header: Dartmouth president Hopkins called him 'a credit
narrative_blocks.body: At John Noyes Colby's funeral on December 10, 1916, Dr. Ernest Hopkins, president of Dartmouth College, represented the faculty and stated: 'a credit to mankind, to his friends and his college, a man to be remembered as a liberalizing influence, whether on the playground, in the fraternity gatherings or in the college classroom.' The church at West Newton Street was full of college students. All pallbearers were from Beta Theta Pi.

education.notes: Class of 1912; largest class to that date with 102 members; received award for modern languages per research notes.

sources.note: History of Cornelius family; section 3-VI gives Elias Cornelius D.D. line

orbit_connections.name: Elias Boudinot (Gallegina Watie, c.1802-1839)
orbit_connections.relationship: Cornelius selected him for Foreign Mission School Cornwall CT 1818
orbit_connections.notes: Cherokee leader; editor of the Cherokee Phoenix (1828), first Native American newspaper. Signed Treaty of New Echota (1835) ceding Cherokee lands — was assassinated in 1839 by tribal opponents who saw him as a traitor. Named his son Elias Cornelius Boudinot in honor of Cornelius.

orbit_connections.name: Elias Cornelius Boudinot (1835-1890)
orbit_connections.notes: Son of Elias Boudinot; Confederate colonel (2nd Cherokee Mounted Rifles); first Native American admitted to practice before the US Supreme Court; delegate to C.S. House of Representatives from Cherokee Nation 1862-1865. Called "among the great scoundrels of the Gilded Age" by historian Richard White for his railroad lobbying activities. Born in New Echota, Cherokee Nation (now Gordon County, GA); d. Fort Smith, Arkansas 1890. The name Cornelius chose for a Cherokee student rippled through four decades of Cherokee and Confederate history.

orbit_connections.name: John Ridge (c.1802-1839)
orbit_connections.relationship: Cornelius selected him for Foreign Mission School Cornwall CT 1818
orbit_connections.notes: Cherokee political leader; like Boudinot, signed the Treaty of New Echota ceding Cherokee lands; assassinated the same day as Boudinot, June 22, 1839.

marriages.notes: Bidirectional marriage record synced from H01355 in Stage 6Y-268. Originally missing on Elias's side.

institutions.institution_blurb: Secretary of Domestic Correspondence 1819-1832; conducted 1817 Cherokee/Creek tour.

narrative_blocks.body: As ABCFM Secretary of Domestic Correspondence 1819-1832, he was one of the board's most active organizers of Cherokee and Creek mission work. In 1817 he conducted a missionary tour through the Cherokee and Creek nations — helping lay the groundwork for Brainerd Mission. He married Mary Ann Hooker, a 6th-generation double Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Selected Boudinot and Ridge for Cornwall mission school
narrative_blocks.body: Between 1817 and 1818 he traveled a nineteen-month route through Virginia, Tennessee, Natchez, New Orleans and the Cherokee, Creek, and Choctaw nations. He selected Gallegina Watie (Elias Boudinot) and John Ridge for the Foreign Mission School at Cornwall Connecticut — choices that shaped Cherokee political history and contributed to the Trail of Tears.

narrative_blocks.header: First white account of Etowah Indian Mounds
narrative_blocks.body: On the same southern tour he visited the Etowah Indian Mounds in Georgia and wrote the first published account by a white person visiting the site. The Cherokee earthwork would later become a National Historic Landmark.

narrative_blocks.header: Dead five months into ABCFM secretary role
narrative_blocks.body: He was elected Corresponding Secretary of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions in October 1831 — succeeding Jeremiah Evarts. He died at Hartford Connecticut on 12 February 1832, less than five months into the role, at thirty-seven.

narrative_blocks.header: Boudinot named his son for Cornelius
narrative_blocks.body: Elias Boudinot named his son Elias Cornelius Boudinot (1835-1890) in honor of him. That namesake son became a Confederate colonel, the first Native American admitted to practice before the US Supreme Court, and was characterized by historian Richard White as among 'the great scoundrels of the Gilded Age.'

career.organization: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions

sources.note: History of Cornelius family; section 3-VI gives Elias Cornelius D.D. line

notable.notable_blurb: Wife of Elias Cornelius, ABCFM Indian Nations missionary

institutions.institution_blurb: Wife of ABCFM Secretary Rev. Elias Cornelius (X00724), m. 1818.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at thirty-six, lived another forty-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Ann Hooker married Elias Cornelius on September 28, 1818 at Somers, New York — an ABCFM missionary who worked among the Creek and Cherokee nations and published the first scholarly account of the Etowah Indian Mounds. He died in 1832, leaving her widowed at thirty-six. She lived another forty-eight years, dying at Newton Center, Massachusetts in 1880 at age eighty-four.

narrative_blocks.header: A printing error aged her eleven years
narrative_blocks.body: The Cornelius genealogy printed her birth as 1785 — an error her 1818 marriage at twenty-two disproves, and every other record corrects to 1796. Her husband’s first published account of the Etowah Indian Mounds and his choice of Elias Boudinot for the Foreign Mission School are part of the household story she carried for forty-eight widowed years.

orbit_connections.name: Joseph Harvey (Yale 1809) — succeeded Asahel at Goshen
orbit_connections.relationship: Harvey succeeded Asahel Hooker as pastor at Goshen CT in 1810
orbit_connections.notes: Yale Biographical Sketches (Dexter): "The Rev. Asahel Hooker (Yale 1789) was dismissed from his pastorate over the Congregational Church of Goshen, in Litchfield County, on June 12, 1810; and Mr. Harvey thereafter supplied the pulpit with such favor that he was unanimously called to the vacant place." Also: Joseph Edwards (Yale, graduated c.1806) "began the study of theology upon graduation with the Rev. Asahel Hooker (Yale 1789), of Goshen, at the same time conducting an academy there." Asahel Hooker's Goshen pastorate was evidently a center of theological training in Litchfield County during the early 19th century. (Yale Biographical Sketches of Grads 1805-1815, Dexter 1912; URL: https://www.cga.ct.gov/hco/books/Biographical_Sketches_of_Grads_of_Yale_1805-1815_1912.pdf)

notable.notable_blurb: pastor of the church in Goshen until dismissed

narrative_blocks.header: Eighteen years at Goshen, dismissed for ill health
narrative_blocks.body: Asahel Hooker was ordained at Goshen, Connecticut on September 7, 1791, and served the congregation as beloved pastor for eighteen years. He was dismissed June 12, 1810 — not over doctrine or congregational conflict, but because his health had broken. Throughout his Goshen tenure he was a published voice in the Connecticut Evangelical Magazine.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Jonathan Edwards's granddaughter Phebe Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: On June 11, 1792 he married Phebe Edwards (H00940), daughter of Hon. Timothy Edwards and granddaughter of Rev. Jonathan Edwards. Like Asahel, Phebe descended from Thomas Hooker — but through Mary Hooker Pierpont's line rather than the surname line. Their son Edward William Hooker would later write the 1849 biography of Thomas Hooker cited as the foundational source by Edward Hooker's 1909 Descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: His son wrote the 1849 Thomas Hooker biography
narrative_blocks.body: His son Rev. Edward William Hooker (H01354) wrote The Life of Rev. Thomas Hooker in 1849. The biography became the foundational source for nineteenth-century Hooker scholarship and was cited as a primary reference in Edward Hooker's 1909 Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker. Edward William inherited the double Hooker descent — surname line through Asahel, Edwards line through his mother Phebe.

education.notes: — Yale College 1789. Son of Asahel Hooker (H00262). Congregational pastor at Goshen CT 1791-1810, Norwich CT 1812-1813. Yale Biographical Sketches mention him as the pastor at Goshen when Joseph Harvey (Yale 1809) succeeded him and when Joseph Edwards (Yale student) studied theology with him.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
